3.5.a.info:
FHRP (First Hop Redundancy Protocol) is the general family name for any protocol that removes the single point of failure at the default gateway. The 'first hop' is the first router a host sends a packet to when leaving its local subnet — and without redundancy, if that router dies, every host on the subnet loses all access to remote networks, including the internet.

The problem FHRP solves: PCs, servers, phones, and IoT devices almost always have a single default gateway IP configured (either statically or via DHCP). If that gateway goes down, those hosts have no other way to forward off-subnet traffic. You could configure two gateways manually on every host, but operating systems still typically use only the primary — and reconfiguring thousands of hosts after a failure is impractical. FHRP solves this by making two or more routers share one virtual gateway address, so hosts only ever know about that one virtual IP.

The three FHRPs tested on the CCNA: HSRP (Cisco proprietary, Active/Standby), VRRP (open standard RFC 5798, Master/Backup), and GLBP (Cisco proprietary, the only one that actively load-balances across routers). All three share the same core idea — one virtual IP, one virtual MAC, transparent failover — but differ in terminology, defaults, and capabilities.

Why this matters in real networks: Core and distribution-layer routers almost always run FHRP. A typical enterprise campus has redundant distribution switches acting as HSRP or VRRP pairs, one per VLAN's SVI. User traffic flows through the Active/Master router; if it fails, the Standby/Backup takes over within 10 seconds (HSRP) or 3 seconds (VRRP) and users often don't even notice. Without FHRP, a single reload or line-card failure would black-hole an entire VLAN.

params.title: FHRP protects the default gateway
params.shield: Virtual IP + Virtual MAC (shared between 2+ routers)

hack.memory: FHRP = 'Family of protocols that Heal the default gateway if Router Perishes.' Three family members: HSRP (Cisco, Active/Standby), VRRP (open, Master/Backup), GLBP (Cisco, active-active load balancer). They all solve ONE problem: hosts only know one gateway IP, so that IP must never truly 'belong' to a single router.
hack.practice: Draw the before/after: (1) Before FHRP — one router at 10.0.0.1 as gateway. Router dies, 200 PCs lose internet. (2) After FHRP — R1 (10.0.0.2) and R2 (10.0.0.3) share virtual IP 10.0.0.1. PCs still use 10.0.0.1. R1 dies, R2 takes 10.0.0.1, PCs keep working. Drawing this twice cements why FHRP exists before you memorize any protocol details.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 opens with exactly this 'why FHRP' framing. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. The exam tests concept-level recall: 'Which technology eliminates a single point of failure at the default gateway?' = FHRP. Always understand the problem first — the protocol-specific details only stick when you know why they exist.

3.5.a.info:
The Virtual IP (VIP) is the shared default-gateway IP address that hosts actually configure. It does not belong to any single router's physical interface — instead, it 'floats' between the routers in the FHRP group, always held by whichever router is currently Active/Master.

How hosts use the VIP: A PC is configured with default gateway 10.0.0.1 (the VIP). When it needs to send a packet off-subnet, it ARPs: 'Who has 10.0.0.1?' The currently Active router responds with the virtual MAC. The PC frames the packet with that virtual MAC as the destination, and the Active router forwards it. The PC never knows (or cares) which physical router is behind the VIP.

Addressing rules per protocol: In HSRP, the VIP must be different from any participating router's interface IP — typically you use .1 as the VIP and assign .2 and .3 to the physical routers. In VRRP, the VIP can equal an existing interface IP; the router whose physical IP matches becomes the IP Address Owner with automatic priority 255. In GLBP, the VIP is shared but the MAC returned in ARP replies varies by host (that's how load balancing works).

Why this design works: Because the VIP is bound to whichever router is Active, failover is purely an internal routing decision between the two routers. The hosts see no changes — same gateway IP, same gateway MAC, same ARP cache entries. Failover is completely transparent to Layer 3 endpoints, which is the entire point of FHRP.

hack.memory: VIP = the 'badge' of the gateway — one badge, two officers, only the one currently on duty wears it. Hosts ARP for the badge (VIP), never for the officer's real name (physical IP). Rule of thumb: in HSRP the VIP is a brand-new IP (like .1); in VRRP it CAN equal one router's real IP; in GLBP one VIP fronts multiple MACs.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer with HSRP configured: on a PC, run 'arp -a' after pinging 10.0.0.1. Note the MAC it learned — that's the virtual MAC of whichever router is Active. Shut down that Active router's interface and wait 10 seconds. Ping 10.0.0.1 again — still works, and the MAC in 'arp -a' is still the same virtual MAC (now carried by the other router). That proves the VIP-plus-virtual-MAC abstraction is what hosts actually use.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. The exam will ask 'What IP address do hosts configure as their default gateway in an HSRP deployment?' Answer: the virtual IP — never a physical router interface IP. Miss this and you miss the entire FHRP concept.

3.5.a.info:
The virtual MAC address is the Layer 2 counterpart to the virtual IP. Just like the VIP, it is shared among FHRP group members and 'floats' with the Active/Master role. Without a shared virtual MAC, failover would still require every host to re-ARP for the VIP — causing a noticeable outage. With a shared virtual MAC, ARP caches remain valid across failovers.

Why the virtual MAC is critical: When a host wants to send off-subnet, it encapsulates the packet in an Ethernet frame with destination MAC = virtual MAC (learned from an ARP reply). If only the IP floated and each router had its own MAC, every host's ARP cache would point to the dead router's MAC after failover — frames would go nowhere until the ARP entry aged out (typically 4 hours on Windows). By sharing a virtual MAC, the new Active router immediately accepts frames destined for the same MAC and continues forwarding.

Format per protocol (memorize these):

  • HSRPv1: 0000.0C07.ACXX — the last octet XX is the HSRP group number in hex. Group 1 = 0000.0C07.AC01, Group 10 = 0000.0C07.AC0A.
  • HSRPv2: 0000.0C9F.FXXX — the last three hex digits encode the group, supporting the v2 range up to 4095.
  • VRRP: 0000.5E00.01XX — XX is the VRRP group/VRID in hex.
  • GLBP: 0007.B400.XXYY — XX is the group, YY is the AVF number (1–4).

Gratuitous ARP on failover: When the Standby/Backup takes over, it sends a gratuitous ARP announcing 'I now own the virtual MAC on this port' — this updates every upstream switch's CAM table so frames are forwarded to the new Active router's port immediately. This is what makes HSRP/VRRP failover feel instant at the switch layer.

hack.memory: Virtual MAC = same story as VIP but at L2 — it floats with the Active role so host ARP caches stay valid. Memorize the 4 formats by their 'fingerprint prefix': HSRPv1 = 0000.0C07.AC, HSRPv2 = 0000.0C9F.F, VRRP = 0000.5E00.01, GLBP = 0007.B400. If you see a MAC starting with those bytes in a packet capture, you know which FHRP is running before you read anything else.
hack.practice: Configure HSRP group 5 in Packet Tracer. Run 'show standby' and confirm the virtual MAC ends in .AC05 (hex 5 = 05). Change to group 16 — MAC should end in .AC10 (hex 10 = 16 decimal). Then enable 'standby version 2' and watch the MAC switch to the 0000.0C9F.FXXX format. Doing this hands-on fixes the formats in your memory better than any flashcard.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 walks through the virtual MAC format. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. The exam loves to show a packet capture or 'show standby' output and ask 'what group number is this?' or 'which FHRP is this?' — those four prefixes are the fastest way to answer.

3.5.a.info:
The CCNA expects you to instantly differentiate the three FHRPs by terminology, vendor, defaults, and transport. You will not configure all three — but you will be shown output or a scenario and asked 'which FHRP is this?' The fastest way to answer is pattern recognition on the fingerprint differences.

HSRP — Hot Standby Router Protocol: Cisco proprietary. Uses Active/Standby terminology. Runs over UDP 1985. Multicast 224.0.0.2 (v1) or 224.0.0.102 (v2). Hello 3s, Hold 10s. Default priority 100, range 0–255. Preemption OFF by default. VIP must differ from any router's physical IP.

VRRP — Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol: Open standard (RFC 5798). Uses Master/Backup terminology. Runs as IP protocol 112 (not UDP!). Multicast 224.0.0.18. Advertisement 1s, Master-down typically 3s. Default priority 100, range 1–254, and 255 reserved for the IP address owner. Preemption ON by default. VIP can equal a router's physical IP.

GLBP — Gateway Load Balancing Protocol: Cisco proprietary. Uses AVG/AVF terminology. Runs over UDP 3222. Multicast 224.0.0.102. The only FHRP that actively load-balances — up to 4 AVFs forward simultaneously under a single VIP. Virtual MAC 0007.B400.XXYY.

Quickest exam shortcut: See 'Active/Standby' → HSRP. See 'Master/Backup' → VRRP. See 'AVG' or 'AVF' or 'load-balance mode' → GLBP. See UDP 1985 → HSRP. See IP protocol 112 → VRRP. See 224.0.0.18 → VRRP.

hack.memory: Three FHRPs, three fingerprints: HSRP = 'Hot Standby' (Cisco, polite, preempt off). VRRP = 'Very Assertive' (open, preempt on, IP owner rules). GLBP = 'Groups Load-Balance Properly' (Cisco, active-active). If you only remember three differences, make them: (1) terminology Active/Master/AVG, (2) preempt default off/on/per-role, (3) vendor Cisco/open/Cisco.
hack.practice: Build a 3-column table on paper: HSRP | VRRP | GLBP. Rows: terminology, vendor, transport, multicast, hello/advert, preempt default, priority default/range, virtual MAC prefix, load-balancing y/n. Fill it in from memory, then verify. This one table answers 80% of the FHRP exam questions you'll see.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 ends with exactly this comparison table. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18 table 18-X is the canonical reference. Expect a 'which FHRP uses Master/Backup terminology?' question and a 'which FHRP can load-balance?' question — both are free points if you've burned in the comparison.

3.5.a.info:
Transparent failover is the defining feature of FHRP. From the hosts' perspective, nothing changes when the Active router dies — same gateway IP, same gateway MAC, same ARP cache entry, same TCP sessions (mostly). The switch layer re-learns the new port for the virtual MAC via gratuitous ARP, and traffic continues.

The failover sequence (HSRP example): (1) R1 is Active, sending Hellos every 3 seconds. (2) R1's interface goes down — it stops sending Hellos. (3) R2 (Standby) notices three missed Hellos — after the 10-second Hold timer expires, R2 declares R1 dead. (4) R2 transitions to Active state, assigns the virtual IP and virtual MAC to its interface, and sends a gratuitous ARP (GARP) saying 'virtual MAC is now reachable via my port.' (5) Upstream switches update their CAM tables immediately from the GARP. (6) Hosts' ARP caches are already correct — they've been pointing at the virtual MAC the entire time. Traffic resumes.

What the host does during failover: absolutely nothing. No DHCP renewal, no ARP flush, no reboot. The host was not even aware there was a failure. This is why FHRPs are 'first hop' protocols — they handle redundancy at the very first hop so the rest of the stack can stay blissfully unaware.

Caveat — in-flight packets: Packets in flight during the 10-second dead interval (HSRP) or 3-second dead interval (VRRP) are dropped. TCP will retransmit and recover, but UDP-based real-time protocols (voice, video) may experience a brief glitch. Tuning timers faster (sub-second hellos) can reduce this but increases CPU load and false failovers on congested links. Most enterprises accept the default timers as a reasonable trade-off.

hack.memory: Transparent failover = the magic of FHRP. Host never touches its config. The Standby quietly becomes Active, assumes the virtual IP + virtual MAC, and announces itself to the switches via gratuitous ARP. If you ever hear 'but the host would have to re-ARP!' — no, it wouldn't, because the MAC is the SAME virtual MAC that was always in the cache.
hack.practice: Continuous-ping from a PC to 8.8.8.8 through an HSRP pair. Shut R1's HSRP interface while ping is running. Count the dropped pings — typically 3-5 (matches the 10-second hold timer). Then bring R1 back — zero drops on recovery (unless preemption kicks in with another brief outage). Running this once and watching the counter move makes failover real.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. The exam might ask 'what must hosts reconfigure after HSRP failover?' — the answer is NOTHING. Another favorite: 'what mechanism does the new Active router use to notify switches of its new role?' — gratuitous ARP. Know both.

3.5.b.info:
HSRP (Hot Standby Router Protocol) is Cisco's original FHRP, released in the mid-1990s and still the most commonly deployed gateway redundancy protocol in Cisco-dominant enterprises. It's Cisco proprietary — you won't find it on a Juniper, Arista, or generic Linux router. If your environment is all Cisco, HSRP is the default choice because of its long track record and tight IOS integration.

Role terminology (memorize exactly): One router is Active — it holds the virtual IP, responds to ARP, forwards all traffic. One router is Standby — it monitors the Active via Hellos and is ready to take over. Any additional routers in the same group sit in Listen state — they know about the group but are not 'next in line.' If 5 routers are in the same HSRP group, only ONE is Active and only ONE is Standby; the other three are Listen.

HSRP state machine: Initial → Learn → Listen → Speak → Standby → Active. Most of these transitions happen quickly at startup. The states you'll see in 'show standby' output are Listen, Speak, Standby, and Active. 'Speak' means the router is actively participating in the election but hasn't settled into Standby or Active yet.

Multiple groups on one interface: HSRP supports MHSRP (Multi-group HSRP) — different VLANs (or different groups on the same VLAN) can have different Active routers, letting you load-share manually. Example: VLAN 10 = R1 Active, R2 Standby. VLAN 20 = R1 Standby, R2 Active. Both routers carry traffic, and failover still works. This is the Cisco way of doing load distribution without moving to GLBP.

hack.memory: HSRP = 'Hollywood Stunt-double Router Protocol' — Cisco-only, Active is the star, Standby is the stunt double waiting in the trailer. 'Hot Standby' literally means 'the backup is warm and ready to go.' Only Active forwards traffic; Standby just watches Hellos. MHSRP = poor man's load-balancing by putting different Actives on different VLANs.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: two routers on a shared switch, both with HSRP group 1 VIP 10.0.0.1 on their LAN interface. Configure R1 priority 110 and R2 priority 100. Run 'show standby' on both — R1 should be Active, R2 Standby. Bonus: add HSRP group 2 with the priorities reversed for a second VLAN/SVI to practice MHSRP, and watch both routers carry traffic simultaneously.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. The exam will test terminology (Active/Standby = HSRP) and vendor (Cisco only). If asked 'which FHRP would you use in a mixed-vendor environment?' the answer is VRRP, not HSRP — because HSRP only runs on Cisco.

3.5.b.info:
The HSRP virtual MAC format is the single most-memorized fact about HSRP. It appears on the exam as a 'identify the group number from this MAC' question or 'which FHRP is this capture from?' question.

HSRPv1 format: 0000.0C07.ACXX

  • 0000.0C — Cisco's registered OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier).
  • 07.AC — the HSRPv1 marker. Any MAC that starts with 0000.0C07.AC is HSRPv1, no exceptions.
  • XX — the HSRP group number, in hexadecimal. Group 0 = 00, group 10 = 0A, group 100 = 64, group 255 = FF.

HSRPv2 format: 0000.0C9F.FXXX — the last three hex digits encode the group. This format is necessary because HSRPv2 supports groups 0–4095, which won't fit in two hex digits.

Worked examples: HSRPv1 group 1 = 0000.0C07.AC01. Group 15 = 0000.0C07.AC0F. Group 200 = 0000.0C07.ACC8 (200 decimal = C8 hex). HSRPv2 group 1000 = 0000.0C9F.F3E8 (1000 decimal = 3E8 hex).

How hosts learn this MAC: A host ARPs for the virtual IP. The Active router replies with 'the virtual IP is at virtual MAC 0000.0C07.AC01' — NOT its physical interface MAC. The host caches this virtual MAC and uses it as the destination for all off-subnet frames. On failover, the new Active router starts accepting frames destined to this same virtual MAC — the host's ARP cache never needs to change.

hack.memory: HSRPv1 virtual MAC = '0000.0C07.AC + group-in-hex.' Break it down: 0000.0C = Cisco. 07.AC = 'HSRP.' XX = group number in hex. So if you see 0000.0C07.AC05 in a capture, it's HSRPv1 group 5. v2 swaps in 0000.0C9F.FXXX because it needs more room for groups up to 4095. Practice hex conversion for common group numbers: 10=0A, 16=10, 100=64, 255=FF.
hack.practice: Configure HSRP group 100 and group 255 in Packet Tracer. Run 'show standby' on each — confirm the MACs end in .AC64 and .ACFF respectively. Then switch to HSRPv2 with 'standby version 2' and verify the MAC format changes to 0000.0C9F.FXXX. Five minutes of this burns the format in permanently.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Guaranteed exam territory: 'A network engineer sees this virtual MAC in a capture: 0000.0C07.AC14. Which FHRP and which group?' Answer: HSRPv1, group 20 (0x14 = 20 decimal). Convert hex confidently and you get these for free.

3.5.b.info:
HSRP Hello messages use a very specific transport that the CCNA exam tests directly. If the question gives you a port/protocol and asks 'which FHRP?', transport is often the fastest answer.

HSRP transport facts (memorize):

  • Protocol: UDP.
  • Port: 1985 (source and destination).
  • Multicast destination — HSRPv1: 224.0.0.2 (the 'all routers on this subnet' multicast address).
  • Multicast destination — HSRPv2: 224.0.0.102 (a dedicated HSRPv2 multicast so it doesn't collide with other protocols using 224.0.0.2).
  • TTL: 1 (Hellos are never forwarded off the local subnet — they're strictly a same-segment protocol).

What flows over this transport: Hello messages (every 3 seconds) containing the sender's priority, state, and authentication (if configured). Coup messages (when a preempting router seizes the Active role). Resign messages (when the Active gracefully steps down). Everything is inside a single UDP packet on port 1985.

Why HSRPv2 uses a different multicast: 224.0.0.2 is reserved for 'all routers,' and many protocols listen on it. This creates CPU load and potential conflicts. HSRPv2 moved to 224.0.0.102 — a dedicated address — to isolate HSRP traffic and allow IPv6 operation (which v1 doesn't support).

Distinguishing from VRRP: VRRP does NOT use UDP — it uses IP protocol 112 directly. If a capture shows UDP/1985, it's HSRP. If it shows an IP header with protocol number 112 and no L4 header, it's VRRP. This is a favorite exam trick.

hack.memory: HSRP transport = 'UDP 1985, multicast 224.0.0.2 (v1) or 224.0.0.102 (v2).' Mnemonic: '1985 — the year of the Hot Standby.' If the question says 'IP protocol 112' or '224.0.0.18' it's NOT HSRP, it's VRRP. If it says 'UDP 3222' it's GLBP. Match the port to the protocol like a lineup.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer Simulation mode, filter for HSRP packets. Click one and confirm: Ethernet dst = 01:00:5E:00:00:02, IP dst = 224.0.0.2, IP TTL = 1, UDP dst port = 1985. Each field is a potential exam question and you've now observed each one with your own eyes.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Classic exam question: 'Which transport does HSRP use?' Answer: UDP 1985. And 'what multicast address does HSRPv2 use?' Answer: 224.0.0.102 (not the v1 224.0.0.2). Easy points if you've memorized.

3.5.b.info:
HSRP uses two timers that determine how quickly failover happens: the Hello timer and the Hold (dead) timer. The defaults are conservative — prioritizing stability over fast failover.

Default values:

  • Hello timer: 3 seconds. The Active router sends a Hello multicast every 3 seconds. The Standby does the same.
  • Hold timer: 10 seconds. If the Standby doesn't hear a Hello from the Active for 10 seconds (roughly three missed Hellos), it declares the Active dead and takes over.

The 3x Hello rule: By convention, Hold ≥ 3× Hello. Cisco's default (3/10) technically isn't exactly 3×, but it's close enough and provides one extra second of grace before failover. This is deliberate — fast timers catch failures quickly but cause 'false positive' failovers on brief network hiccups (congestion, CPU spikes, STP convergence).

Tuning timers: For faster convergence, you can configure sub-second timers: standby 1 timers msec 200 msec 700 (Hello 200ms, Hold 700ms). This gives sub-second failover but increases control-plane load and failover sensitivity. Most enterprises keep the defaults unless they have voice/video SLAs that require faster recovery.

Timer mismatch behavior: If R1 is configured with 3/10 and R2 with 1/3, they can still form a relationship. The Active router's advertised timers take effect for the group — whoever wins the Active election dictates the timing. However, intentionally mismatched timers are almost always a configuration error and should be cleaned up.

Practical failover time: With defaults, total outage is ~10 seconds from the moment R1 fails until R2 is Active and forwarding traffic. Sub-second tuning brings this under 1 second. For 'five 9's' SLAs, pair fast timers with BFD or use redundant L3 paths instead.

hack.memory: HSRP timers = '3 and 10 — Hello 3, Hold 10.' Dead ≈ 3× Hello by rule of thumb. Compared to VRRP's 1-second advertisement, HSRP is deliberately slower — Cisco's designers prioritized stability over speed. Tune with 'standby [grp] timers msec X msec Y' if you need sub-second failover.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, shut the Active's HSRP interface and time the failover by hand (or use continuous ping and count drops). You should see ~10 seconds of dropped traffic. Then configure 'standby 1 timers msec 200 msec 700' on both routers, retest — now <1 second of drops. Feel the difference between default and tuned.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam will test: 'What are the default Hello and Hold timers for HSRP?' Answer: 3 seconds and 10 seconds. Know the 3× rule of thumb. Contrast with VRRP's 1-second advertisement.

3.5.b.info:
HSRP priority is the primary tiebreaker for deciding which router becomes Active. It's a numeric value from 0 to 255, with a default of 100, and higher priority wins.

Election rules (in order):

  1. Highest priority wins Active. Priority 110 beats priority 100.
  2. If priorities tie, highest interface IP address on the HSRP interface wins. So if both routers are default priority 100, the one with IP 10.0.0.3 beats the one with IP 10.0.0.2.

How to change priority: standby [group] priority [0-255] under the interface. Example: standby 1 priority 110 sets the priority to 110 for group 1 on that interface. Typical convention: set the 'preferred' router to 110 (or higher) and leave the backup at default 100 — this makes failover behavior deterministic and easy to reason about.

Priority 0 is special: A router sets its own priority to 0 when it wants to actively yield Active role — usually because of interface tracking (a key uplink went down and the router decided it's not a good primary gateway anymore). Priority 0 means 'I will never be Active in this group.' When you see a router at priority 0 in 'show standby,' check whether a tracked object is down.

Combined with preemption: Priority alone does NOT force the higher-priority router to be Active after failover — preemption (covered in 3.5.f) is the separate knob that controls reclaim behavior. A common trap: configure R1 with priority 110 but forget standby 1 preempt. After a failover, R1 comes back but stays Standby (priority 110) while R2 continues as Active (priority 100). The 'higher priority' router is not the Active router — that's not a bug, it's the default behavior.

hack.memory: HSRP priority = higher wins, default 100, range 0-255, tie broken by highest interface IP. Convention: make the preferred Active 110. Priority 0 means 'I'm out — my tracked object failed.' Remember: priority picks WHO should be Active, but PREEMPTION decides whether the higher-priority router reclaims the role after recovery. Two separate knobs.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: configure R1 and R2 both with default priority 100. Check 'show standby' — the one with higher interface IP is Active. Now set R1 to 110 and reload HSRP — R1 becomes Active. Change R1's tracked uplink to fail (if configured) and watch R1 drop to priority 90 or 0 — R2 takes over. This hands-on sequence explains priority better than any text.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam: 'Two HSRP routers both at default priority. Which becomes Active?' Answer: the one with the higher interface IP. Also 'what is the default HSRP priority?' = 100. And 'what priority range does HSRP use?' = 0-255 (wider than VRRP's 1-254).

3.5.b.info:
This is probably the most-tested single fact about HSRP. Preemption is OFF by default in HSRP. That means even if you set R1 to priority 110 and R2 to priority 100, once R1 has failed and R2 has taken over, R1 will NOT automatically reclaim the Active role when it recovers. R1 returns as Standby, R2 remains Active, and your 'preferred' router sits idle until an admin intervenes.

Enabling preemption: standby [group] preempt under the interface. That's it. Once configured, a higher-priority router will immediately seize the Active role upon return via a Coup message — a special HSRP message type that says 'I'm taking over because I have higher priority.'

Why Cisco chose 'off' as the default: Preempt-on can cause an oscillating failover if the primary router is flapping (coming up and down repeatedly). Each time it comes up, it seizes Active; each time it fails, Standby takes over again. This preempt-churn can thrash the network. Leaving preempt off makes HSRP 'sticky' — whoever is Active stays Active until they fail, which is conservative but safe.

Why VRRP chose 'on' as the default: VRRP's designers assumed admins would set priority to indicate a preferred Master — if that Master returns, the admin's intent is clearly 'make it Master again.' VRRP optimizes for matching admin intent; HSRP optimizes for stability. Both are defensible — just know which default applies where.

Preempt delay: You can add a delay to preemption so the returning router waits before seizing Active: standby [group] preempt delay minimum 60 gives the returning router 60 seconds to stabilize before reclaiming. This is useful for routers that take a while to finish booting routing protocols after the HSRP interface comes up — without delay, the router would become Active while still learning routes, black-holing traffic briefly.

states: With preempt: R1 sends Coup, reclaims Active ✓

hack.memory: HSRP preempt = OFF by default. Classic exam trap: higher-priority router comes back, does NOT reclaim Active unless you explicitly enabled preempt. Mnemonic: 'HSRP is polite — Honored Standby, Respects Possession.' VRRP is assertive — preempt ON. Always pair priority config with 'standby [grp] preempt' unless you have a reason not to.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer absolute must-do lab: R1 pri 110, R2 pri 100, virtual IP 10.0.0.1, no preempt configured. Shut R1 → R2 becomes Active. Bring R1 back → R1 stays Standby (!!!). Add 'standby 1 preempt' under R1's interface. Shut/unshut R1 again → R1 reclaims Active via Coup message. Do this once and you will NEVER miss a preemption question again.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 specifically demonstrates this. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. 100% test target: 'Router has priority 120, current Active has priority 100. Which becomes Active after the higher-priority router recovers?' Answer depends on preempt. If not mentioned as configured, assume default (off) → the priority-100 router stays Active.

3.5.b.info:
The HSRP configuration commands are concise and live entirely under the interface. You configure HSRP on each router's LAN interface (or SVI on an L3 switch) that is participating in the group. The CCNA expects you to read and, in some exam variants, type these commands.

Minimum config (both routers):

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip address 10.0.0.2 255.255.255.0   ! physical IP — different per router
 standby 1 ip 10.0.0.1               ! virtual IP — same on both

Full recommended config (Active side):

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip address 10.0.0.2 255.255.255.0
 standby 1 ip 10.0.0.1
 standby 1 priority 110              ! higher than default 100
 standby 1 preempt                    ! reclaim after failure
 standby 1 name CORE-VLAN10           ! optional label
 standby version 2                    ! switch to HSRPv2 (optional)

Key command breakdown:

  • standby [group] ip [vip] — REQUIRED. Enables HSRP on the interface and sets the VIP.
  • standby [group] priority [0-255] — sets priority. Default 100.
  • standby [group] preempt — enables preemption. OFF by default.
  • standby [group] timers [hello] [hold] — tune Hello/Hold. Use msec for sub-second.
  • standby [group] track [object] — reduce priority if a tracked object goes down.
  • standby [group] authentication [key] — optional, rarely used on CCNA.
  • standby version {1|2} — switch HSRP version. v1 is default.

Verification: show standby (detailed output), show standby brief (compact summary across all groups — this is what you actually use day-to-day). The brief output shows interface, group, priority, state (Active/Standby/Listen), Active router, Standby router, and virtual IP in a single line each.

hack.memory: Every HSRP command starts with 'standby [group]'. The ONE required command is 'standby [grp] ip [vip].' Everything else is optional. Memorize the quartet: 'standby X ip, standby X priority, standby X preempt, standby X track.' Verify with 'show standby brief' — the compact one-liner output is what you'll see on the exam.
hack.practice: Type-it-from-memory drill: open a blank Packet Tracer router. From scratch, configure HSRP group 1 with VIP 10.0.0.1, priority 110, preempt, and HSRPv2. Do this 3 times in a row without looking at notes. If you can type it cold, you're exam-ready on HSRP config. Then practice reading 'show standby brief' until the columns feel natural.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 has the full config walkthrough. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam configures and verifies HSRP — be fluent in both. The single command that will appear in output-interpretation questions is 'show standby brief' — practice reading it.

3.5.c.info:
HSRPv1 supports HSRP groups numbered 0 to 255. For many enterprises, 256 possible groups is plenty — but in large campus environments with hundreds or thousands of VLANs, 256 groups simply isn't enough. HSRPv2 addresses this by expanding the group range to 0 to 4095.

Why this matters in practice: A common convention is to match the HSRP group number to the VLAN number for easy mental mapping. If you have VLAN 2000, you'd use HSRP group 2000. With HSRPv1 capped at 255, this convention breaks the moment you cross into VLAN 256+. HSRPv2's range of 4095 covers the full standard 802.1Q VLAN range (1–4094), letting you keep the group-matches-VLAN convention even in huge networks.

The reason for the expansion: HSRPv1 encodes the group in a single byte (XX in the virtual MAC 0000.0C07.ACXX). A byte holds 0–255. HSRPv2 allocates three hex digits in the virtual MAC (0000.0C9F.FXXX), giving 12 bits = 4096 possible values (0–4095). This is a direct consequence of the MAC format change, not a separate feature.

Enabling HSRPv2: standby version 2 under the interface. Once enabled, you can configure groups up to 4095. If you try to configure group 300 without first switching to v2, IOS will reject it with an error.

hack.memory: HSRPv1 groups: 0-255 (1 byte). HSRPv2 groups: 0-4095 (12 bits, 3 hex digits). The range limit is a direct consequence of how many bits are allocated in the virtual MAC. 4095 conveniently covers the full 802.1Q VLAN range of 1-4094, enabling the common 'HSRP group = VLAN ID' convention.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, try 'standby 500 ip 10.0.0.1' without switching to v2 — it gets rejected. Add 'standby version 2' first, retry — accepted. Small but memorable demonstration of the range limit.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 mentions the group range difference. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam: 'What is the maximum HSRP group number in HSRPv1?' = 255. 'In HSRPv2?' = 4095. Easy recall points.

3.5.c.info:
HSRPv2 uses a different virtual MAC format than HSRPv1 to accommodate its larger group number range. While v1 encodes the group in a single byte, v2 uses three hex digits.

HSRPv2 virtual MAC format: 0000.0C9F.FXXX

  • 0000.0C — Cisco OUI (same as v1).
  • 9F.F — the HSRPv2 marker. This replaces the v1 07.AC marker and uniquely identifies v2 traffic.
  • XXX — the HSRP group number in hex, 000 to FFF (0–4095 decimal).

Worked examples: HSRPv2 group 1 = 0000.0C9F.F001. Group 10 = 0000.0C9F.F00A. Group 256 = 0000.0C9F.F100. Group 4095 = 0000.0C9F.FFFF.

Identification tactic: In a packet capture or 'show' output, look at the first six hex digits of the virtual MAC. 0000.0C07.AC = HSRPv1. 0000.0C9F.F = HSRPv2. These prefixes are your fingerprint — no further context needed to tell the versions apart.

Why a new marker was needed: The v1 MAC allocated 2 hex digits (1 byte) for the group. Stretching that to 3 digits would have required eating into the 07.AC marker bytes, which were already fixed. Designers chose a clean new marker (9F.F) and three digits of group space, giving 4096 groups with no ambiguity.

hack.memory: HSRPv2 MAC = '0000.0C9F.F + 3-digit hex group.' The 9F.F is the v2 fingerprint — see it in a capture, you're looking at HSRPv2. Trivia worth remembering: v1's AC in 07.AC arguably encoded 'Active' (not officially, but useful memory hook), and v2 broke that pattern for a cleaner marker.
hack.practice: Configure 'standby version 2' in Packet Tracer with group 100. Run 'show standby' — confirm MAC is 0000.0C9F.F064 (hex 64 = decimal 100). Now try group 1000 — MAC should be 0000.0C9F.F3E8 (hex 3E8 = decimal 1000). Verifying hex→decimal for a couple of groups locks the format in.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam may show a MAC and ask 'which HSRP version?' — use the first 6 hex digits (the prefix) to decide. v1 vs v2 is purely a prefix-recognition problem.

3.5.c.info:
HSRPv2 uses a dedicated multicast address instead of the generic 'all routers' address used by v1. This separation helps isolate HSRPv2 traffic from other protocols and is required for HSRPv2's IPv6 support.

Multicast addresses:

  • HSRPv1 (IPv4): 224.0.0.2 — the 'all routers on this subnet' link-local multicast. Shared with many other protocols (it's the generic router address).
  • HSRPv2 (IPv4): 224.0.0.102 — a dedicated address. Not shared with other protocols.
  • HSRPv2 (IPv6): FF02::66 — the IPv6 equivalent for HSRPv2 Hellos over IPv6.

Why dedicate a multicast for v2: The address 224.0.0.2 is used by many protocols that need to reach 'all routers on this subnet.' Every router on the segment processes every frame sent to 224.0.0.2, even if it's not running HSRP, because it might be running some other protocol that uses that address. This creates CPU overhead and potential for confusion. HSRPv2's 224.0.0.102 is only processed by HSRPv2 speakers, reducing unnecessary work.

Coincidence alert: GLBP also uses 224.0.0.102 but on UDP port 3222, while HSRPv2 uses UDP port 1985. The multicast address alone isn't enough to differentiate — you need the transport/port pair. Capture tools show both clearly.

hack.memory: HSRPv1 multicast = 224.0.0.2 (generic 'all routers'). HSRPv2 multicast = 224.0.0.102 (dedicated). IPv6 equivalent for v2 = FF02::66. Memory hook: 'v2 uses 102 — a hundred more than v1's 2, and shared with GLBP on a different port (3222).'
hack.practice: Run a Packet Tracer capture with both v1 and v2 configured (separate labs). Filter by destination IP. Confirm v1 packets go to 224.0.0.2 and v2 packets go to 224.0.0.102. The visual difference is all you need to internalize it.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam: 'What multicast address does HSRPv2 use for Hello messages over IPv4?' Answer: 224.0.0.102. Same answer pops up as distractor for GLBP — check the UDP port to disambiguate.

3.5.c.info:
One of the most important reasons to run HSRPv2 instead of v1 is IPv6 support. HSRPv1 is IPv4-only — it literally cannot provide gateway redundancy for IPv6 hosts. HSRPv2 adds a parallel IPv6 capability, letting you run HSRP on dual-stack networks.

How IPv6 HSRP differs: Instead of a single virtual IPv4 address, HSRPv2-for-IPv6 provides a virtual link-local address that hosts use as their default gateway (IPv6 hosts typically use link-local addresses for on-link communication including default gateway). The mechanism (election, preemption, Hello/Hold timers) is identical to IPv4 HSRPv2 — only the address family differs.

Configuration: standby version 2 is still required, then standby [group] ipv6 [virtual-link-local-address] instead of standby [group] ip. You can have the same group number running both IPv4 and IPv6 on the same interface.

Multicast for IPv6: HSRPv2 Hellos travel on FF02::66 when running over IPv6. This is a link-local multicast (scope 2), so Hellos never leave the local segment.

Why v1 doesn't support IPv6: v1's frame format and MAC allocation were designed before IPv6 was a concern. Retrofitting IPv6 into v1 would have broken backward compatibility with existing deployments. Cisco chose to bundle all v2 changes (larger groups, dedicated multicast, IPv6) into a single version bump.

hack.memory: HSRPv1 = IPv4 only. HSRPv2 = IPv4 + IPv6. If the exam mentions IPv6 and HSRP in the same sentence, the answer must involve HSRPv2. For IPv6 HSRP: command uses 'ipv6' keyword, multicast is FF02::66, virtual IP is typically a link-local address.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer has limited HSRPv2-for-IPv6 support — but on a real IOS router with 'ipv6 unicast-routing' enabled, you can configure 'standby 1 ipv6 FE80::1' after enabling 'standby version 2.' Worth trying if you have access to real gear or GNS3/CML.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam question pattern: 'Your network is transitioning to IPv6. Which FHRP version supports IPv6?' Answer for HSRP specifically: HSRPv2. Note: VRRPv3 also supports IPv6 — VRRPv2 does not.

3.5.c.info:
HSRPv1 and HSRPv2 are NOT interoperable. All routers in an HSRP group must run the same version. Mixing versions causes neighbor relationships to fail, and both routers end up thinking they're Active (a dual-Active scenario that breaks forwarding for hosts).

Why they can't interoperate: They use different virtual MAC formats (0000.0C07.ACXX vs 0000.0C9F.FXXX), different multicast destinations (224.0.0.2 vs 224.0.0.102), and different Hello packet structures. A v1 router listening on 224.0.0.2 will never see a v2 router's Hellos sent to 224.0.0.102 — they're silently invisible to each other. Both assume the other router doesn't exist and both claim Active role.

The silent-failure trap: A version mismatch doesn't generate a clear error. show standby on each router will show itself as Active (because it never hears Hellos from the other one). Hosts arping for the VIP may get two different virtual MACs from two different routers at unpredictable times, causing intermittent connectivity. Troubleshooting is painful because there's no obvious 'mismatch' log.

Migration strategy: To upgrade from v1 to v2 without downtime, you'd typically (1) ensure all routers in the group are running IOS versions that support v2, (2) configure a maintenance window, (3) change standby version 2 on all routers as close to simultaneously as possible — typically via config replace or automation. During the brief transition window, some traffic may be dropped.

Default is v1: Without standby version 2, IOS defaults to HSRPv1. So if you configure 'standby 500 ip 10.0.0.1' on a router, IOS will reject it (because 500 > 255 = v1 limit) until you also add 'standby version 2.' That rejection is a useful accidental reminder that 'v1 is the default.'

hack.memory: HSRPv1 and HSRPv2 do NOT interoperate. All routers in a group MUST match version. Silent failure — no clean error, just dual-Active chaos. If failover isn't working and 'show standby brief' shows both routers as Active, check the version first. Default is v1; 'standby version 2' is required to enable v2.
hack.practice: Deliberately misconfigure in Packet Tracer: R1 default (v1), R2 with 'standby version 2.' Observe that both routers show themselves as Active in 'show standby' — neither sees the other. Fix by matching versions, confirm normal Active/Standby operation resumes. This is the fastest way to see silent-failure behavior.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam: 'What happens if two routers in an HSRP group are configured with different versions?' Answer: they fail to form a relationship and both may become Active. Know that version mismatch is a silent failure, not a logged error.

3.5.d.info:
VRRP (Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol) is the IETF open-standard FHRP, defined in RFC 5798 (VRRPv3 for IPv4 and IPv6). It provides the same gateway redundancy as HSRP but is vendor-neutral — you can run VRRP between a Cisco router and a Juniper, Arista, or Linux router using the same standard.

When to choose VRRP over HSRP: Any mixed-vendor environment. If your network has any non-Cisco L3 devices (common in cloud, multi-vendor enterprises, or ISP edges), VRRP is the right choice because HSRP simply won't run on non-Cisco hardware. Even in all-Cisco shops, some teams standardize on VRRP for portability — the choice is easier if they think they might introduce a second vendor later.

Versions: VRRPv2 (RFC 3768) — IPv4 only. VRRPv3 (RFC 5798) — supports both IPv4 and IPv6. Modern Cisco IOS typically runs VRRPv3 by default. The CCNA treats VRRP generically; you don't need to memorize which version adds IPv6 as deeply as you do for HSRP.

Key design differences from HSRP (to memorize): Uses Master/Backup terminology (not Active/Standby). Preempt is ON by default (opposite of HSRP). Virtual IP CAN match a router's physical IP (the 'IP address owner'). Runs over IP protocol 112 directly (not UDP). Multicast is 224.0.0.18. Advertisements default to 1 second (faster than HSRP's 3s).

params.title: VRRP — Open Standard FHRP (RFC 5798)
params.shield: Master/Backup with shared VIP — works across Cisco, Juniper, Arista, Linux

hack.memory: VRRP = 'Very Routable Redundancy Protocol' (open standard, RFC 5798). Terminology: Master/Backup. Use VRRP in mixed-vendor networks — HSRP only runs on Cisco. Remember the big four differences from HSRP: Master/Backup terms, preempt ON default, VIP can match physical, IP proto 112 (not UDP).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer (which supports VRRP on some router models): configure 'vrrp 1 ip 10.0.0.1' on two routers. Run 'show vrrp' — note the role is 'Master,' not 'Active.' Test failover by shutting the Master's interface; the Backup promotes to Master within 3 seconds (3× 1s advertisement). The faster timer versus HSRP is tangible.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 covers VRRP alongside HSRP. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam almost always includes one VRRP question, usually comparing terminology or preempt defaults. Being able to instantly say 'Master/Backup = VRRP' is the fastest disambiguation.

3.5.d.info:
VRRP uses the terms Master and Backup instead of HSRP's Active/Standby. The roles work the same way, but the vocabulary difference is one of the fastest ways to tell the two protocols apart on the exam.

Master: The router currently holding the virtual IP, responding to ARP for the VIP, and forwarding traffic. Exactly one Master per VRRP group. Election criteria: highest priority wins; ties broken by highest interface IP address.

Backup: Any router in the group that is not currently Master. Backups monitor the Master's advertisements and stand ready to take over. Unlike HSRP's three-role model (Active/Standby/Listen), VRRP has just two roles: Master and Backup. Every non-Master router is simply a Backup.

Exam pattern recognition: The CCNA exam frequently presents a command output or configuration snippet and asks 'which FHRP is running?' If the output contains the word 'Master' (or 'Backup'), the answer is always VRRP. If it contains 'Active' (or 'Standby'), the answer is always HSRP. GLBP uses 'AVG' and 'AVF' — distinct from both. These three terminology sets are non-overlapping, so terminology alone disambiguates.

Why the different terminology: VRRP was designed as an open standard and its authors deliberately avoided HSRP's trademarked terminology. 'Master/Backup' is a more common generic term for primary/secondary role, while 'Hot Standby' was Cisco's marketing term for HSRP. Functionally identical, just differently named.

hack.memory: VRRP = Master/Backup. HSRP = Active/Standby. GLBP = AVG/AVF. Three protocols, three non-overlapping vocabulary sets. Remember: 'MASTER has a big M for Main gateway. BACKUP = the spare.' See 'Master' anywhere in exam output — it's VRRP, full stop.
hack.practice: Flip between 'show standby brief' (HSRP) and 'show vrrp brief' (VRRP) output in Packet Tracer or on real gear. Identify the role column for each — it will say 'Active' or 'Standby' for HSRP, 'Master' or 'Backup' for VRRP. Three sessions of this drill makes terminology automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. The single most common VRRP exam question is terminology-based. 'Which FHRP uses the terms Master and Backup?' = VRRP. Gift points.

3.5.d.info:
This is the direct opposite of HSRP, and it's a guaranteed exam question. VRRP preemption is ON by default. If you configure R1 with priority 110 and R2 with priority 100, R1 becomes Master. If R1 fails, R2 takes over. When R1 recovers, R1 automatically reclaims Master via VRRP's preempt mechanism — no additional configuration required.

Why VRRP chose 'on' as the default: The designers of VRRP assumed that if an administrator bothered to set explicit priorities, they clearly want the higher-priority router to be Master whenever available. Automatically reclaiming the role matches that admin intent. HSRP chose 'off' for stability — VRRP chose 'on' for intent-match. Both defaults are defensible; the CCNA just wants you to know which is which.

Disabling VRRP preemption: no vrrp [group] preempt under the interface. Rarely done, but useful when you have a flapping primary router and want to stop the thrash.

The 'IP address owner' always preempts: If a router's physical interface IP matches the VRRP virtual IP, that router is the IP Address Owner and is automatically assigned priority 255 (the maximum). Priority 255 cannot be beaten. As long as the IP Address Owner is up, it is Master — preemption is effectively mandatory for that router, regardless of the preempt setting on other routers in the group.

The exam trap: Two scenarios — 'HSRP preempt' and 'VRRP preempt' — look identical on the surface but have opposite defaults. Read the question carefully. If it says HSRP, default is off. If it says VRRP, default is on. Get them backward once and you'll get the question wrong; get them right and it's free points.

states: VRRP preempt ON (default): R1 reclaims Master immediately ✓

hack.memory: VRRP preempt = ON by default (assertive: 'I'm back, I'm in charge'). HSRP preempt = OFF by default (polite: 'you can keep it'). The mnemonic: 'VRRP → V for Vigorous → preempt ON. HSRP → H for Humble → preempt OFF.' Memorize the opposites and you own both exam questions.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure VRRP on R1 (priority 110) and R2 (priority 100). Verify R1 is Master. Shut R1 → R2 becomes Master. Bring R1 back → R1 AUTOMATICALLY reclaims Master (no extra config needed — that's preempt ON by default). Compare against the HSRP version of the same lab where you had to manually add 'standby preempt.' The two labs side-by-side lock in the default difference.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam guaranteed: 'Which FHRP has preemption enabled by default?' = VRRP. 'Which has it disabled?' = HSRP. Also know: the IP owner (priority 255) always takes Master regardless of preempt settings on others.

3.5.d.term: VRRP virtual IP can match physical
3.5.d.info:
VRRP has a unique capability that HSRP does not: the virtual IP can be identical to one of the participating router's physical interface IP addresses. When this happens, that router becomes the IP Address Owner and is automatically assigned priority 255, effectively guaranteeing it will be Master whenever it's operational.

Example scenario: R1's physical IP on the LAN is 10.0.0.1/24. You configure VRRP group 1 with virtual IP 10.0.0.1 — matching R1's physical IP. R1 is now the IP Address Owner. R2 (physical IP 10.0.0.2) also joins the VRRP group. R1 automatically runs at priority 255; R2 runs at default priority 100. R1 is always Master when up. If R1 fails, R2 becomes Master and responds to 10.0.0.1 with the VRRP virtual MAC — but once R1 recovers, it reclaims Master immediately (because priority 255 > priority 100 AND preempt is on by default).

Why allow the VIP to match a physical IP: In some deployments, the 'preferred' gateway is a specific router with specific capabilities (e.g., it has the WAN uplink), and admins want hosts to literally use that router's IP. VRRP honors this by making the IP Address Owner the de-facto preferred Master. When it's up, it's Master. When it's not, another router steps in — but the IP address seen by hosts is the same.

Priority 255 is reserved: You cannot manually set a VRRP router's priority to 255. Priority 255 is automatically assigned only to the IP Address Owner. Any attempt to configure vrrp 1 priority 255 on a non-IP-owner will be rejected. This is a VRRP protocol rule from RFC 5798.

HSRP cannot do this: In HSRP, the virtual IP must be different from every participating router's physical IP. There is no equivalent of the 'IP Address Owner' role in HSRP. This is one of the key differentiators the exam tests.

children.name: Becomes Master only if R1 fails

hack.memory: VRRP unique feature: VIP can match a physical IP. Match = IP Address Owner → automatic priority 255 → always Master. HSRP cannot do this; its VIP must always be a fresh address. If exam says 'the virtual IP is the same as a router's interface IP,' the answer is VRRP — and that router is the IP Owner with priority 255.
hack.practice: Configure in Packet Tracer: R1 physical 10.0.0.1, R2 physical 10.0.0.2, VRRP group 1 VIP 10.0.0.1 on both. Run 'show vrrp' on R1 — priority shows 255. On R2 — priority 100. Shut R1 → R2 becomes Master (priority 100, just a temporary holder). Bring R1 back → R1 reclaims Master (priority 255 wins every time). Observe the automatic priority assignment.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Classic exam question: 'What priority is automatically assigned to the VRRP IP Address Owner?' = 255. 'Which FHRP allows the virtual IP to equal a physical router IP?' = VRRP (not HSRP).

3.5.d.info:
VRRP does not use UDP — it runs directly over IP as IP protocol number 112. This is a distinctive detail the exam loves because it's the easiest way to tell VRRP apart from HSRP and GLBP in a packet capture.

VRRP transport facts (memorize):

  • Protocol: IP (directly — no UDP/TCP).
  • IP protocol number: 112 (assigned by IANA specifically for VRRP).
  • Multicast destination: 224.0.0.18 (the VRRP-specific multicast address).
  • TTL: 255 (required by RFC — any VRRP packet received with TTL ≠ 255 MUST be dropped as a security measure; this prevents VRRP packets from being forwarded across routers).
  • IPv6 VRRPv3: still IP protocol 112, multicast FF02::12.

Why no UDP: Skipping UDP saves 8 bytes per packet and aligns with how other control-plane protocols work (OSPF also runs directly over IP as protocol 89; EIGRP uses protocol 88). Control protocols don't need L4 services like multiplexing — they have their own well-defined semantics and a single designated IP protocol number.

Comparison table:

  • HSRP — UDP 1985, multicast 224.0.0.2 (v1) or 224.0.0.102 (v2).
  • VRRP — IP protocol 112, multicast 224.0.0.18.
  • GLBP — UDP 3222, multicast 224.0.0.102.

Identification shortcut: If a packet capture shows IP protocol 112 with no L4 header, it's VRRP. If it shows UDP 1985, it's HSRP. If it shows UDP 3222, it's GLBP. Three unique fingerprints, three different protocols.

layers.label: VRRP
layers.content: No UDP layer — VRRP rides directly on IP

hack.memory: VRRP transport = 'IP protocol 112, multicast 224.0.0.18, TTL 255.' NO UDP. That's the distinctive fingerprint. Mnemonic: '112 for VRRP — V is the 22nd letter, squinted 22 looks like 112? It's a stretch. Easier: just memorize 112.' Compare: HSRP = UDP 1985 · 224.0.0.2. GLBP = UDP 3222 · 224.0.0.102. Three unique pairs.
hack.practice: Wireshark or Packet Tracer capture: filter for 'vrrp.' Confirm the IP header protocol field shows 112 and there's no UDP layer above it — VRRP fields sit directly in the IP payload. Capture HSRP too and verify you can see a UDP header on port 1985. Three minutes of capture inspection permanently differentiates the two.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam: 'Which FHRP uses IP protocol 112?' = VRRP. 'Which uses UDP 1985?' = HSRP. These are rapid-fire recall questions — memorize the three port/protocol pairs.

3.5.d.info:
VRRP sends Advertisement packets at a default interval of 1 second — three times faster than HSRP's 3-second Hello. This gives VRRP faster convergence without sub-second tuning.

Default timers:

  • Advertisement interval: 1 second. Master sends an advertisement every second.
  • Master-down interval (skew time + 3× advertisement): by default roughly 3 seconds, meaning after 3 missed advertisements a Backup declares the Master dead and promotes itself.
  • Skew time: a small fraction based on priority, ensuring that when multiple Backups detect Master failure, higher-priority Backups start the election slightly ahead — deterministic tiebreaking.

Failover time: With defaults, VRRP failover takes roughly 3 seconds — still much faster than HSRP's 10-second default. For sub-second failover, you can tune: vrrp 1 timers advertise msec 200 for a 200ms advertisement interval.

Why VRRP is faster by default: RFC 5798 designers optimized for IPv6 deployments where fast convergence is more important (routing protocols like OSPFv3 rely on fast gateway failover). Cisco's HSRP predates VRRP and kept 3-second Hellos to match the conservative defaults of other Cisco protocols.

Common tuning scenarios: For voice/video workloads, admins often tune both HSRP and VRRP to sub-second intervals. VRRP starts from a lower baseline so the same 'fast' target requires less tuning aggressiveness.

hack.memory: VRRP advertisement = 1 second. Master-down ≈ 3 seconds. Compare HSRP Hello 3s / Hold 10s. VRRP is 3× faster out of the box. Mnemonic: 'VRRP = Very Responsive, 1 second.' HSRP = Hot Standby, 3 seconds (Cisco's traditionally conservative defaults).
hack.practice: Run identical failover tests on Packet Tracer (HSRP vs VRRP) with default timers. Time the ping-drop count. HSRP drops ~10 pings, VRRP drops ~3. The 3x speed difference is tangible. Record it once and the timer defaults will stick.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam: 'What is the default VRRP advertisement interval?' = 1 second. Contrast with HSRP Hello = 3 seconds. Recall also the 3× rule for Master-down interval.

3.5.d.info:
VRRP priority is functionally similar to HSRP priority but has different range rules because of the IP Address Owner's special priority 255.

VRRP priority facts:

  • Range: 1–254 (configurable).
  • Default: 100.
  • Priority 0: Reserved — a router sends priority 0 advertisements to immediately step down and force a Backup election (like HSRP priority 0 for tracking).
  • Priority 255: Reserved for the IP Address Owner. Cannot be manually configured on a non-owner router.
  • Higher priority wins Master election. Ties broken by highest interface IP (same as HSRP).

Contrast with HSRP range: HSRP range is 0–255 — admins can manually configure priority 255 because HSRP has no 'IP owner' concept. VRRP's 1–254 range is narrower because 0 and 255 are both reserved for protocol signaling (step-down and IP-owner respectively).

Configuration: vrrp [group] priority [1-254] under the interface. Example: vrrp 1 priority 110 sets priority to 110. To revert to default: no vrrp 1 priority returns it to 100.

Interaction with preemption: Because VRRP preempt is ON by default, whichever router has the highest priority (which is always the IP owner if present) will be Master whenever it's up. The only time priority really matters in a plain VRRP deployment is without an IP owner, when multiple routers all have priority 100 (tiebreak goes to highest interface IP).

hack.memory: VRRP priority: range 1-254, default 100. Priority 255 = IP Owner only (can't manually set). Priority 0 = 'I'm stepping down, elect someone else.' HSRP priority 0-255, VRRP 1-254. The range difference is entirely because of the IP Address Owner rule — VRRP carves out 255 exclusively for the owner.
hack.practice: On real IOS or Packet Tracer: try configuring 'vrrp 1 priority 255' on a non-owner router. IOS rejects it. Try 'vrrp 1 priority 254' — accepted. That rejection message is a memorable anchor for the 1-254 range rule.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam: 'What is the valid priority range for a non-IP-owner VRRP router?' = 1-254. 'What priority does the IP Address Owner have?' = 255.

3.5.e.info:
GLBP (Gateway Load Balancing Protocol) is Cisco's proprietary FHRP that provides both gateway redundancy AND automatic load balancing across multiple routers. It is the only FHRP tested on the CCNA that forwards traffic through multiple routers simultaneously by default — HSRP and VRRP only use one Active/Master at a time.

The core problem GLBP solves: In HSRP and VRRP, if you have two routers, one sits idle as Standby/Backup while the other carries 100% of traffic. That's wasted capacity. Workarounds like MHSRP (different Actives on different VLANs) help but require manual configuration. GLBP solves it natively: both routers forward traffic at the same time for the same VLAN under a shared virtual IP.

How load balancing works: One router is elected AVG (Active Virtual Gateway) — it answers all ARP requests for the virtual IP. Unlike HSRP/VRRP, the AVG does NOT always return the same MAC. Instead, it cycles through up to four different virtual MACs, each owned by a different AVF (Active Virtual Forwarder). Host A gets MAC-A (→ R1). Host B gets MAC-B (→ R2). Host C gets MAC-C (→ R3). Now three routers are actively forwarding traffic.

Transport and formats: UDP port 3222, multicast 224.0.0.102, virtual MAC format 0007.B400.XXYY (XX = group, YY = AVF number 01–04).

Exam scope: GLBP appears as 1–2 questions on the CCNA, mostly conceptual — what it is, AVG/AVF roles, the fact that it load-balances, vendor (Cisco). You do NOT need to configure GLBP for the CCNA. Just understand the concept.

params.title: GLBP — Redundancy + Load Balancing
params.shield: AVG distributes up to 4 MACs → up to 4 AVFs forward simultaneously

hack.memory: GLBP = 'Gateway Load Balancing Protocol' — the only FHRP that actively load-balances by default. Cisco proprietary. AVG hands out different MACs in ARP replies; AVFs forward the traffic. Up to 4 AVFs. UDP 3222, multicast 224.0.0.102. If the exam says 'load-balancing FHRP,' the answer is GLBP.
hack.practice: Draw the concept on paper: VIP 10.0.0.1, three routers R1/R2/R3. Three hosts ARP for 10.0.0.1. AVG (say R1) responds to Host A with MAC-A (R1), Host B with MAC-B (R2), Host C with MAC-C (R3). Draw arrows from each host through the correct router. Seeing the traffic split visually is what makes GLBP click.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 covers GLBP at a conceptual level. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam: 'Which FHRP provides active-active load balancing?' = GLBP. 'Is GLBP Cisco proprietary or open standard?' = Cisco proprietary. Don't overstudy configuration — it's not tested.

3.5.e.info:
The AVG (Active Virtual Gateway) is the 'controller' of a GLBP group. It doesn't necessarily forward the most traffic — its key job is to respond to ARP requests for the virtual IP and assign different virtual MACs to different hosts. This ARP-level load distribution is the magic that makes GLBP load-balance without any host-side cooperation.

AVG responsibilities:

  1. Elected via priority. Like the HSRP Active or VRRP Master, the AVG is the highest-priority router in the group. Default priority is 100, range 1–255 (note: GLBP range differs slightly from HSRP/VRRP).
  2. Assigns virtual MACs to AVFs. The AVG allocates up to 4 virtual MACs (numbered 01 through 04) and assigns each to a different AVF. These MACs are the ones the AVG hands out in ARP replies.
  3. Answers ARP requests for the VIP. When a host ARPs, the AVG returns a virtual MAC based on its load-balancing algorithm (round-robin, weighted, or host-dependent). Different hosts get different MACs → traffic is distributed across AVFs.
  4. Monitors AVFs. If an AVF fails, the AVG reassigns that virtual MAC to a surviving router so traffic keeps flowing. If the AVG itself fails, a Standby AVG takes over.

AVG is only ONE router at a time. Even though multiple routers forward traffic (as AVFs), only one is the AVG. The AVG role is like an HSRP Active for the control plane, while the AVFs are the data plane workers. In a 3-router GLBP group: R1 is AVG + AVF1, R2 is AVF2 (standby AVG), R3 is AVF3. All three forward traffic, but only R1 answers ARP.

children.name: Responds to host ARPs with load-balanced MACs

hack.memory: AVG = 'the dispatcher.' Only one AVG per group — it's the brain that decides which host talks to which router by handing out different MACs in ARP responses. Elected by priority, highest wins. Acts like HSRP Active for control-plane, but data plane is distributed across AVFs. Remember: AVG does the ARP math, AVFs do the forwarding work.
hack.practice: On a real Cisco device: configure GLBP on R1 and R2 with 'glbp 1 ip 10.0.0.1.' Run 'show glbp' on each. One shows AVG with virtual MAC .XX01 assigned to itself, and virtual MAC .XX02 assigned to the neighbor. Ping from two different hosts — one learns MAC-01, the other MAC-02. Direct observation of ARP-based load distribution makes AVG's role clear.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 explains AVG/AVF. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam: 'What is the role of the AVG in GLBP?' Answer: responds to ARP requests for the VIP and assigns different virtual MACs to different hosts for load distribution. Only one AVG per group.

3.5.e.info:
An AVF (Active Virtual Forwarder) is a router that actively forwards traffic for a specific virtual MAC in a GLBP group. Each AVF is responsible for one virtual MAC (numbered 01 through 04). Up to 4 AVFs per group — this is a hard protocol limit.

Relationship with AVG: The AVG assigns each participating router a virtual MAC (or multiple if fewer than 4 routers exist). Each router becomes the AVF for its assigned MAC. The AVG itself typically also acts as AVF1 (so in a 2-router setup, R1 is AVG + AVF1, R2 is AVF2).

Why 4 is the limit: The GLBP virtual MAC format is 0007.B400.XXYY where XX is the group and YY is the forwarder number. YY is only allocated 2 hex digits (1 byte), and GLBP uses values 01 through 04. Values above 04 are reserved. Hence max 4 AVFs per group.

Failover behavior: If AVF2 fails, AVG notices and reassigns virtual MAC-02 to a surviving router (say AVF1 temporarily takes over MAC-02). Hosts that had learned MAC-02 now have their traffic forwarded through AVF1 — without re-ARPing, because the MAC didn't change; only the router behind it changed. This is similar to how the primary VIP 'floats' in HSRP/VRRP, but applied per-MAC.

Primary vs Secondary AVF: For each virtual MAC, one router is the primary AVF (normally owns that MAC) and one or more are secondary AVFs (take over if the primary fails). This gives GLBP per-MAC redundancy in addition to cross-group redundancy (AVG election).

right.label: AVF (up to 4 per group)
items: Forwards traffic for its assigned MAC

hack.memory: AVF = 'the forwarder.' Up to 4 AVFs per GLBP group (limited by 2-hex-digit YY in the virtual MAC 0007.B400.XXYY). Each AVF owns one virtual MAC and forwards all traffic destined to that MAC. Think of AVG as dispatcher, AVFs as delivery drivers — one dispatcher (AVG), up to 4 drivers (AVFs), each with their own route.
hack.practice: In a 3-router GLBP lab, run 'show glbp' — you'll see virtual MACs .XX01, .XX02, .XX03 each assigned to a different router. Fail one (shut its interface) and watch 'show glbp' — the lost MAC gets reassigned to a surviving AVF. Hosts that learned the failed MAC continue working because the MAC itself survives (just moves to a new forwarder).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam: 'What is the maximum number of AVFs in a GLBP group?' = 4. 'What does an AVF do?' = forwards traffic for a specific assigned virtual MAC.

3.5.e.info:
GLBP supports three load-balancing algorithms that control how the AVG hands out virtual MACs in ARP responses. Choose based on desired distribution behavior.

1. Round-robin (default): The AVG cycles through virtual MACs in order. Host A gets MAC-01, Host B gets MAC-02, Host C gets MAC-03, Host D gets MAC-04, Host E gets MAC-01 again, and so on. Result: even distribution across AVFs assuming hosts generate roughly equal traffic. Best general-purpose choice.

2. Weighted: Each AVF is assigned a weight. The AVG hands out MACs proportionally to the weights. If R1 has weight 100 and R2 has weight 50, R1 gets 2x as many hosts as R2. Useful when one router has more bandwidth or processing capacity than another — you can bias the distribution accordingly.

3. Host-dependent: The AVG hashes the requesting host's MAC address to consistently assign it the same virtual MAC every time. Host A always gets MAC-01, Host B always gets MAC-02. If Host A drops off and comes back, it gets MAC-01 again. Useful for stateful forwarding (e.g., NAT or firewall state) where traffic for a given host must always go through the same router to avoid asymmetric-path problems.

Configuration: glbp [group] load-balancing {round-robin | weighted | host-dependent} under the interface. Default is round-robin. Weight for weighted mode: glbp [group] weighting [value].

Exam level: Know the three mode names and their general behavior. You won't be asked to configure GLBP on the CCNA, but you may be asked 'which mode ensures a host always gets the same gateway router?' — host-dependent.

hack.memory: GLBP has 3 load-balance modes: round-robin (default, even split), weighted (proportional to capacity), host-dependent (sticky — same host always gets same router). Mnemonic: 'RWH — Round, Weighted, Host.' If a question mentions stateful forwarding or NAT consistency → host-dependent. Capacity bias → weighted. Default → round-robin.
hack.practice: Flashcard drill: three modes, three one-line descriptions. Quiz yourself: 'which mode for NAT?' = host-dependent. 'Which for routers of different capacities?' = weighted. 'Which by default?' = round-robin. Should take less than 2 minutes to memorize.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 mentions the modes briefly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam may test: 'What is the default GLBP load-balancing mode?' = round-robin. 'Which mode uses a consistent hash of the host MAC?' = host-dependent.

3.5.e.info:
GLBP uses a distinctive virtual MAC format that encodes both the group number and the AVF (forwarder) number.

GLBP virtual MAC format: 0007.B400.XXYY

  • 0007.B4 — the GLBP OUI prefix (assigned to Cisco for GLBP use).
  • 00 — always zero (reserved bits).
  • XX — GLBP group number in hex (01–FF).
  • YY — AVF/forwarder number in hex (01–04; GLBP supports up to 4 AVFs per group).

Worked examples: GLBP group 1, AVF 1 = 0007.B400.0101. Group 10, AVF 3 = 0007.B400.0A03. Group 255, AVF 4 = 0007.B400.FF04.

Transport: GLBP Hellos run on UDP port 3222, multicast destination 224.0.0.102 (same multicast as HSRPv2 but different UDP port — that's how they coexist on the same network without collision).

Fingerprint recognition: If you see a virtual MAC starting with 0007.B4 in a capture, it's GLBP. Compare to HSRPv1 (0000.0C07.AC), HSRPv2 (0000.0C9F.F), and VRRP (0000.5E00.01). Four unique prefixes, four distinct protocols.

Exam weight: This is a low-priority detail. You're much more likely to be asked about AVG/AVF roles or the load-balance modes than the exact virtual MAC format. File this under 'nice to know, unlikely to appear verbatim,' but the 0007.B4 fingerprint is worth knowing for packet-capture identification questions.

segments.label: GLBP OUI prefix
segments.meaning: Assigned by IEEE to Cisco for GLBP

hack.memory: GLBP MAC = '0007.B400.XXYY' where XX = group, YY = AVF number (01-04). The 0007.B4 prefix is the fingerprint. Transport: UDP 3222, multicast 224.0.0.102 (shared with HSRPv2 at the IP level but different UDP port). Low exam priority but memorable if you remember the four FHRP MAC prefixes as a set.
hack.practice: Write all four FHRP MAC prefixes side by side: HSRPv1 = 0000.0C07.AC. HSRPv2 = 0000.0C9F.F. VRRP = 0000.5E00.01. GLBP = 0007.B4. Flashcard this as a single card — 'identify FHRP by MAC prefix' — and you're covered for every capture-based question.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam rarely tests the exact GLBP MAC format — but it may show a MAC prefix and ask 'which FHRP?' The 0007.B4 prefix = GLBP. Bigger-picture understanding (AVG/AVF, load-balancing) matters more for scoring.

3.5.f.info:
Preemption is the behavior that determines whether a higher-priority router reclaims the Active/Master role after it recovers from a failure. Without preemption, a lower-priority router that took over during an outage will continue to own the role even after the original (higher-priority) router returns to service.

The basic scenario: R1 has priority 110, R2 has priority 100. R1 is Active/Master. R1 fails → R2 takes over. R1 recovers. Question: does R1 reclaim the Active/Master role automatically, or does R2 remain as the current Active/Master?

  • Preempt ON: R1 sends a takeover message ('Coup' in HSRP) and immediately reclaims Active/Master. Network returns to its normal 'R1 is primary' state.
  • Preempt OFF: R1 recovers as Standby/Backup. R2 continues as Active/Master despite having lower priority. Network stays in its 'failed-over' state until R2 fails or an admin intervenes.

Per-protocol defaults (the most-tested fact in FHRP):

  • HSRP: preempt OFF by default. Enable with standby [group] preempt.
  • VRRP: preempt ON by default. Disable with no vrrp [group] preempt.
  • GLBP: preemption is per-AVG and per-AVF role. AVG preempt is off by default; AVF preemption is on by default.

Why defaults differ: HSRP's designers optimized for stability (no thrashing on a flapping primary). VRRP's designers optimized for admin-intent-matching (if you set higher priority, you want that router to be Master). Both are defensible — the CCNA just requires you know which default applies to which protocol.

hack.memory: Preemption = 'does the higher-priority router TAKE BACK the role after recovery?' On = yes, take it back. Off = no, leave it alone. The defaults flip between protocols — HSRP off, VRRP on. Think of it as the single knob that can flip a failover scenario from 'primary is Master again' to 'backup is still Master.' Always check the preempt setting before answering any failover question.
hack.practice: Run the same failover sequence twice in Packet Tracer — once with preempt, once without. With preempt: original Active reclaims role on recovery. Without: secondary stays as Active. Do this in both HSRP and VRRP configs. The visual difference between the two outcomes is what makes preemption stick.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. The single most-tested FHRP concept. Expect at least one preempt-related question on the exam, usually phrased as 'Which router is Active/Master after scenario X?' — answer depends entirely on whether preempt is configured or the protocol's default.

3.5.f.info:
HSRP preempt is OFF by default. This one fact is responsible for more CCNA exam mistakes than almost any other FHRP concept. Memorize it: HSRP = preempt OFF unless explicitly enabled.

Scenario walk-through:

  1. You configure HSRP group 1 on R1 (priority 110) and R2 (priority 100). Virtual IP 10.0.0.1.
  2. R1 becomes Active (higher priority). R2 becomes Standby. Normal operation.
  3. R1's interface fails. R2 detects 3 missed Hellos (Hold timer = 10s) and becomes Active.
  4. 10 minutes later, R1's interface comes back up.
  5. Question: Who is Active now?

Answer without preempt (default): R2 remains Active. R1 becomes Standby. Even though R1 has higher priority (110 > 100), preempt is OFF so R1 does NOT reclaim. The current Active 'wins' by default.

Answer with preempt (standby 1 preempt on R1): R1 sends a Coup message and immediately reclaims Active. R2 drops back to Standby.

Why Cisco chose off: Consider a router with a flaky uplink that cycles up-down every 60 seconds. If preempt were on, every time the router came up it would seize Active, then lose it on the next flap. Traffic would thrash through different routers repeatedly. HSRP off-by-default prevents this scenario — the current Active 'sticks' until it truly fails.

Best practice for production: Enable preempt on the preferred primary router, combined with a preempt delay to prevent flap-churn: standby 1 preempt delay minimum 60 makes the router wait 60 seconds of stable uptime before reclaiming.

hack.memory: HSRP preempt = OFF by default. Write that sentence 10 times. Then remember: if the exam mentions HSRP and doesn't mention 'preempt' being configured, assume OFF — the higher-priority router does NOT reclaim. Enable with 'standby [grp] preempt.' Pair with 'preempt delay minimum 60' in production to avoid flap-churn.
hack.practice: The single most important FHRP lab: two routers in HSRP, priorities 110 and 100, NO preempt. Shut/unshut the priority-110 router. Confirm it stays Standby after recovery. Then add 'standby 1 preempt' under its interface. Shut/unshut again — now it reclaims Active. Running this once permanently fixes the default behavior in memory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 explicitly demos this. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. 'Which router is Active in HSRP after the original Active recovers?' is a guaranteed question. Answer depends on preempt — default = the lower-priority router stays Active.

3.5.f.info:
VRRP preempt is ON by default. Exact opposite of HSRP. In VRRP, a higher-priority router will automatically reclaim the Master role when it recovers — no configuration needed.

Scenario walk-through:

  1. R1 (priority 110) and R2 (priority 100) in VRRP group 1. Virtual IP 10.0.0.1.
  2. R1 is Master. R2 is Backup.
  3. R1 fails. R2 promotes to Master.
  4. R1 recovers. Because preempt is ON by default, R1 immediately reclaims Master. R2 drops back to Backup.

No configuration required to get this behavior — it's the default. In fact, the only way to prevent preemption in VRRP is to explicitly disable it with no vrrp [group] preempt, which is rarely done.

The exception — IP Address Owner: If a router's physical IP matches the VRRP virtual IP, that router is the IP Address Owner with priority 255. Priority 255 always wins, and because preempt is on by default, the IP Owner will always be Master when up. You cannot suppress this with 'no preempt' — priority 255 overrides the non-preempt request as a protocol-level special case.

Why VRRP chose on: The IETF designers assumed that explicit priority configuration implies admin intent: 'I want this router to be Master when it can.' Automatically reclaiming matches that intent. HSRP chose off for stability; VRRP chose on for intent-matching. Both are valid design choices — just know which is which.

Practical impact: VRRP 'feels' more responsive during recovery because the primary reclaims immediately. HSRP 'feels' more stable because the current Active sticks. Neither is universally better — it's a trade-off, and vendor defaults reflect different priorities.

states: Default preempt ON: R1 reclaims Master ✓
Only 'no vrrp 1 preempt' could prevent this (rare)

hack.memory: VRRP preempt = ON by default. Opposite of HSRP. Higher-priority router automatically reclaims Master on recovery, no config needed. To disable: 'no vrrp [grp] preempt' — rarely done. Pair this knowledge with HSRP's opposite default — if you remember both as a flip pair, you'll never miss the question.
hack.practice: Same lab as the HSRP one, but with VRRP: R1 priority 110 Master, R2 priority 100 Backup, no extra preempt config. Shut R1 → R2 becomes Master. Bring R1 back → R1 reclaims Master AUTOMATICALLY (without 'vrrp 1 preempt' needed). Then try 'no vrrp 1 preempt' on R1 and rerun — R1 stays Backup after recovery. The default difference is obvious side-by-side.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. The HSRP vs VRRP preempt-default flip is one of the top-3 guaranteed exam questions in FHRP. Know both defaults cold.

3.5.f.info:
Understanding why preemption exists and why it's usually enabled in production clarifies many FHRP design questions. The short answer: the 'preferred' router is usually preferred for a real reason (better path, more capacity, closer to the WAN/ISP), and preempt ensures that reason continues to be honored after any failover-recovery cycle.

Typical scenario motivating preempt: Imagine a branch office with two L3 switches. SW1 has a direct 1 Gbps uplink to the ISP. SW2's only uplink is a cheaper 100 Mbps backup circuit. You configure HSRP with SW1 at priority 110 (preferred) and SW2 at 100. Normal operation: SW1 is Active, everyone's traffic flows out the 1 Gbps link.

SW1 reboots for maintenance. SW2 becomes Active. Everyone's traffic now flows over the 100 Mbps backup. Performance degrades but nothing breaks. SW1 comes back. Without preempt, SW2 remains Active and everyone's traffic stays on the slow link until you manually fail it over. With preempt, SW1 reclaims Active immediately and traffic returns to the fast link — the whole point of the priority configuration.

When preempt is NOT desired: If the primary router is unstable (failing repeatedly), preempt-on causes thrashing — every time it comes up, it seizes Active, then loses it on the next flap.
