Traffic oscillates and stability suffers. In this case, leaving preempt off (or adding a long preempt delay) prevents churn while you fix the unstable router.

Preempt delay: standby [group] preempt delay minimum [seconds] (HSRP) gives the recovering router a grace period to stabilize before reclaiming. Common values: 30–300 seconds. This protects against flap-churn without giving up the benefits of preemption on a stable router.

left.label: Preempt ON (typical)
items: Preferred router always Master when up

right.label: Preempt OFF (occasional)
items: Current Master stays until it fails

hack.memory: Preemption exists because the 'preferred' router is preferred for a real reason — usually the best path to the WAN. After failover-and-recovery, you want that preference honored automatically, so preempt brings the preferred router back to Active. Turn it OFF only when the primary is unstable and you want stability over optimization.
hack.practice: Think of preempt as the 'failback automation toggle.' On = automatic failback to primary after recovery. Off = stay on backup until manually switched. This framing connects preempt to real network operations and makes the config decisions intuitive.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam may ask 'why would you enable preemption?' — answer focuses on returning traffic to the preferred (usually best-path) router after recovery. Or 'why use preempt delay?' — answer is preventing flap-churn.

3.5.f.info:
Here is the most common FHRP exam question, presented the way you'll see it on the real CCNA. Master this trap and you've mastered the preemption topic.

Typical question:

R1 and R2 are configured in HSRP group 1. R1 is configured with priority 110; R2 is configured with priority 100. R1 is currently the Active router. R1's HSRP-enabled interface fails, R2 becomes Active. Ten minutes later, R1's interface recovers. Which router is now the Active router?

The trap: Students see 'priority 110 > 100' and assume R1 must be Active. That's wrong. The question says HSRP, which has preempt OFF by default. R1 comes back as Standby because preempt wasn't enabled. R2 is still Active.

How to answer correctly every time: For any FHRP failover question:

  1. Identify the protocol — HSRP or VRRP? (Look for Active/Standby vs Master/Backup.)
  2. Check the preempt default — HSRP off, VRRP on.
  3. Read the question for explicit 'preempt' configuration that overrides the default.
  4. Apply the rule: if preempt is effective (whether by default or configured), higher priority reclaims. If preempt is disabled, current role holder stays.

Variations of the trap: (a) Same scenario with VRRP — answer changes because VRRP preempt is on by default, so R1 reclaims Master. (b) HSRP with 'standby 1 preempt' configured — R1 reclaims Active. (c) HSRP with priority unchanged after a tracking failure — priority might not even be 110 anymore, so election rules may differ.

Memorize the decision tree: HSRP + no preempt configured = current Active stays (even if lower priority). HSRP + preempt = higher priority wins. VRRP + no 'no preempt' configured = higher priority wins (default on). VRRP + 'no preempt' = current Master stays. Four combinations, four answers. Know them cold.

hack.memory: Classic trap: HSRP, higher-priority router recovers, no preempt configured. Answer: the CURRENT Active (lower priority) stays. Preempt is OFF by default in HSRP. Write this sentence on your exam whiteboard before starting: 'HSRP preempt OFF default, VRRP preempt ON default.' Glance at it whenever an FHRP failover question appears. Guaranteed to catch the trap.
hack.practice: Make 5 flashcards, each with a variation: (1) HSRP no preempt, R1 recovers. (2) HSRP with preempt, R1 recovers. (3) VRRP default, R1 recovers. (4) VRRP with no-preempt, R1 recovers. (5) HSRP with preempt delay 60, R1 flaps. Answer each one. If you can nail all 5, you will not miss a preemption question on the exam.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29 specifically calls out this trap. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. This is the most consistently tested FHRP scenario across exam versions. Burn the two defaults (HSRP off, VRRP on) into memory and this trap becomes a free point every time.
